response:
The fare $\eta$ depends directly on the random driving distance $\xi$ in this city. When the distance $\xi$ is less than or equal to 4 kilometers the charge remains a flat rate of 10 yuan. If the distance $\xi$ exceeds 4 kilometers the fare increases by 2 yuan for every additional kilometer. Any fraction of a kilometer beyond the initial 4 kilometers is rounded up to the next whole number. This rounding rule ensures that even a small extra distance triggers the full additional charge for that unit. The relationship can be expressed as $\eta = 10 + 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$ for values of $\xi$ greater than 4. This formula captures the entire pricing structure for the taxi service during the spring season.

A passenger paid 38 yuan for a trip where the actual road distance was 15 kilometers. The base fare covers the first 4 kilometers so the remaining 28 yuan comes from the distance charge. Dividing 28 by the rate of 2 yuan per kilometer shows that the calculated distance used for billing was 14 kilometers beyond the initial 4. This means the total effective distance $\xi$ used for the fare calculation was exactly 18 kilometers. Since the actual driving distance was only 15 kilometers the difference of 3 kilometers must come from waiting time. The city rule states that every 5 minutes of stopping time equals 1 kilometer of distance. Therefore the maximum stopping time is 3 times 5 minutes which equals 15 minutes. This calculation holds true even during the busy spring travel period.
